html:
The Iron Age, according to Thomsen's three-period system, is the period in the prehistory of the Old World that begins when humans learn to extract and process iron ore. In archaeology, the Iron Age is defined as the period when the use of iron becomes prominent in the production of weapons and tools within a society. The Iron Age began at different times in the Middle East, Egypt, Africa, China, and Europe. In the Middle East, ironworking dates back much further. People during the Iron Age made their tools and weapons from iron instead of bronze. Eventually, they transitioned to steel. Jewelry, buckles, and similar items were often made of bronze. The Iron Age ends with the beginning of historical times, i.e., when written records appear. The specific years for this transition vary depending on the region.

In Northern Europe (Scandinavia), the Iron Age traditionally ends with the Viking Age, followed by the Middle Ages, which began with the introduction of Christianity around 1050 AD. This was when Christianity became dominant and more reliable written sources emerged. Before Christianity, a polytheistic religion prevailed, often referred to today as Norse paganism. The transition from the Late Iron Age to the Middle Ages in Scandinavia cannot be precisely dated. From various perspectives, both secular and religious, elements of the Viking Age within the Late Iron Age persisted well into the 12th century, for example, in Sweden.

The early period when iron tools were first produced on a larger scale began around 1500 BC. The introduction of iron led to societal adaptations, changing labor organization, introducing new agricultural methods, altering religious beliefs, and influencing artistic styles. The Iron Age, as an archaeological term, also describes these changes in the relationship between civilization and culture for a people who used iron for tools and weapons.

The concept of the Iron Age provides limited chronological information because not all parts of the world fit neatly into the three-period system. For example, there are regions, such as islands in the Pacific Ocean, inland areas of Africa, and parts of North and South America, where people transitioned directly from using stone tools to iron without an intermediate bronze age. In China, there is no Iron Age because written language and historical records already existed at that time.

The Iron Age is archaeologically characterized by specific types of tools and implements, weapons, personal decorations, and pottery, as well as different systems of decorative design that distinguished it from the Bronze Age. Smiths hammered iron into suitable shapes, and this process was fundamentally different from the methods used to create bronze tools and objects through casting. The decoration on iron tools changed. During the Bronze Age, ornamentation primarily consisted of repetitive patterns. These were replaced by new systems of curved and flowing designs.

== The Beginning of the Iron Age ==

Here's an overview:

  • **What it was:** A period in history marked by the widespread use of iron tools and weapons.
  • **When it started:** Around 750 BC.
  • **Where it happened:** Across Europe, although the exact timing varied from region to region.
  • **Why it's important:** Iron was stronger and more durable than bronze, leading to significant advancements in agriculture, warfare, and other aspects of life.

For more information, visit: [Insert URL here]

Iron from meteorites, an iron-nickel alloy, was used in ancient times by various peoples thousands of years before the Iron Age. The oldest known objects made of meteorite iron are nine small beads dating back to 3200 BC. They were found during excavations of burials in Gerzeh, Lower Egypt, and were shaped through hammering.

The Iron Age begins when mass production of tools and weapons is achieved not only from found iron but also from furnaces where steel alloys with carbon are the raw materials. Only with carbon steel as a product of iron metallurgy can tools or weapons be produced that are harder and lighter than bronze.

Archaeology has identified the beginning of large-scale iron production in Anatolia around 1200 BC, although some archaeological finds suggest an earlier date. Around 3000 BC, iron was a rare and expensive metal in the Near East. The properties of iron compared to bronze were not known at this time. This development may be related to the so-called Late Bronze Age collapse, which occurred during this period. Instability and disruptions in bronze trade may have led to an increase in the use of iron.

Between 1200 BC and 1000 BC, understanding of iron metallurgy increased, and the use of iron objects spread rapidly and widely throughout society. In the history of ferrometallurgy, smelting iron – extracting usable metal from oxidized iron ore – is a much more difficult process than smelting tin and copper. Iron processing involves processes such as smelting with charcoal, further processing through heating and hammering. By chance, the earliest metallurgists may have discovered that steel was formed when iron absorbed charcoal (carbon) on the surface of the metal during this process. While copper and tin, and their alloys (mixtures), can be cold-worked or melted in relatively simple furnaces, such as those used for ceramics, and then cast into molds. Smelting iron requires intense heat treatment with oxygen supplied through bellows and can only be smelted in specially built furnaces. It is not surprising that humans mastered the technology of smelting iron only after several thousand years of bronze metallurgy.

How certain can we be about this?

The Iron Age is largely prehistoric; towards the end, there are occasional foreign references and runes. The picture we have is based on archaeology combined with later sources that look back into the past, so much of it is reconstruction.
tldr: The Iron Age is a period in history when people began using iron instead of bronze to make tools and weapons. This technological advancement allowed for the creation of stronger objects, which fundamentally changed how societies functioned and lived.
lattlast:
The Iron Age begins when people learn to extract iron from the earth and process it into useful objects. Before this, bronze, an alloy of copper and tin, was often used. Because iron was more difficult to melt and work with, special furnaces and techniques were required to obtain the metal. Once the technology spread, it became possible to create tools and weapons that were both harder and lighter than the older bronze items.

The period during which the Iron Age occurred varies depending on the region of the world. In Europe and the Middle East, the use of iron began several thousand years ago, but because there were no written texts from that time, it is difficult to determine exact dates. In Scandinavia, the Iron Age is considered to have ended with the Viking Age, around the year 1050, when Christianity became widespread and more history was recorded in writing.

Iron changed people's daily lives in many ways. It led to new methods in agriculture, altered how work was organized, and gave rise to new artistic styles. Because iron was much more durable than bronze, it became an important foundation for building larger societies and developing new technologies.
